coredata.dc:title: Microplasma-enabled carbon dots composited with multi-walled carbon nanotubes for dopamine detection
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Jiaxin
Xia, Yi
Zou, Zhirong
Yang, Qing
Jiang, Xue
Xiong, Xiaoli
coredata.dc:description: Composited carbon nanomaterials have attracted wide attention and are used for high-sensitivity biological assays due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and excellent electrical conductivity. To further promote electron transfer and enhance electrocatalytic activity to detect dopamine (DA), this study proposed carbon dots (CDs) based on glycerol synthesized by liquid dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) microplasma. Combined with the multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) with excellent electrical conductivity, a composited carbon nanomaterial electrode of CDs/MWCNTs was constructed. As a DA biosensor, the interaction and electron exchange between MWCNTs, CDs, and DA can be enhanced thanks to the π-π stacking force, thereby facilitating the sensitive electrochemical detection of DA. The sensor exhibits good sensing performance toward DA detection with a linear range of 2.0–100 μM, a limit of detection (LOD) of 11.08 nM (S/N = 3), and a sensitivity of 29020 μA cm−2 mM−1. The proposed electrode successfully detected DA levels in human serum samples with satisfactory selectivity and recovery rate. The microplasma-enabled synthesized method provides a promising path for preparing and applying carbon-based nanomaterials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Multiwalled carbon nanotubes
Microplasma
Dopamine
Electrochemical sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a b c) TEM image of CDs, (d) the SAED pattern of CDs, (e) UV–vis (Abs) and fluorescence spectra (Ex, Em) of CDs.

(a) CVs with MWCNTs and CDs/MWCNTs modified GCE without and with 0.1 mM DA, (b) CVs of DA with different concentrations (0–0.5 mM), (c) CV curves of 0.1 mM DA with different pH values (6.2–7.8), (d) Jpa and Epa versus pH, (e) CV curves of 0.1 mM DA with different scan rates from 20 to 200 mV s−1, (f) Jpa and Jpc versus scan rates.
(a) DPVs of DA with different concentrations (2–100 μM), (b) plots of Jpa versus CDA, (c) Jpa of 0.1 mM DA with AA and UA at various concentrations, (d) Reproducibility of electrodes.
Schematic diagram of preparation and DA sensing of CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrodes.
Performance comparison of the CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrode with other reported electrodes for DA detection based MWCNTs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: β-CD: β-cyclodextrin. SNFC: sulfated nanofibrillar cellulose. ta-C: tetrahedral amorphous carbon. Plu- AuNPs: gold nanoparticles-poly(luminol). PTCA: 3,4,9,10-perylene tetra-carboxylic acid. RGO: reduced graphene oxide. HCNTs: helical carbon nanotubes. α-CD: α-cyclodextrin. CNT: carbon nanotube. CE: coated electrodes. EDOT: 3,4-dioxyetylenethiophene. PDDA: poly(diallyl dimethylammonium chloride). GO: graphene oxide. f-MWCNTs: acid functionalized multi-wall carbon nanotubes. SPE: screen printed electrode. GNPs: gold nanoparticles.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The recovery rate of DA in human serum using CDs/MWCNTs/GCE as working electrode.

head.ce:title: Microplasma-enabled carbon dots composited with multi-walled carbon nanotubes for dopamine detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610068, Sichuan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610068, Sichuan, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Composited carbon nanomaterials have attracted wide attention and are used for high-sensitivity biological assays due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and excellent electrical conductivity. To further promote electron transfer and enhance electrocatalytic activity to detect dopamine (DA), this study proposed carbon dots (CDs) based on glycerol synthesized by liquid dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) microplasma. Combined with the multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) with excellent electrical conductivity, a composited carbon nanomaterial electrode of CDs/MWCNTs was constructed. As a DA biosensor, the interaction and electron exchange between MWCNTs, CDs, and DA can be enhanced thanks to the π-π stacking force, thereby facilitating the sensitive electrochemical detection of DA. The sensor exhibits good sensing performance toward DA detection with a linear range of 2.0–100 μM, a limit of detection (LOD) of 11.08 nM (S/N = 3), and a sensitivity of 29020 μA cm−2 mM−1. The proposed electrode successfully detected DA levels in human serum samples with satisfactory selectivity and recovery rate. The microplasma-enabled synthesized method provides a promising path for preparing and applying carbon-based nanomaterials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is considered an essential neurotransmitter in the human body with a concentration range of approximately 10−7–10−3 mol/L [1], which plays a critical role in the central nervous system, cardiovascular, kidney, and hormone systems. The abnormal metabolism of DA can lead to diseases such as Parkinson's disease, hyperactivity disorder, and schizophrenia [2]. Therefore, the determination of DA content has always been a significant concern in the medical field, and it is critical to establish a rapid and sensitive method for detecting trace DA. At present, researchers have developed various methods for the detection of DA, including fluorescence spectrometry [3], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [4], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [5], liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC-MS) [6] and so on. However, compared to the method mentioned above, due to the high electrochemical oxidation activity of DA, electrochemical methods have attracted great attention due to their simple operation and sensitive results. Additionally, DA, uric acid (UA), and ascorbic acid (AA) always coexist in organisms, and the oxidation potentials (Epa) of DA, AA, and UA are similar, resulting in overlapping voltammetry responses, which is not conducive to the specific detection of DA in vivo [7–9].

Carbon nanomaterials, like multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) and carbon dots (CDs), are widely used for environmental and biological assays due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, simple synthesis, low cost, and good electrical conductivity. MWCNTs and their derivatives serve as both and conductive substrates to support other nanomaterials; there have been many reports suggesting that glassy carbon electrodes (GCE) decorated with MWCNTs-based nanomaterials can discriminately detect DA, AA and UA such as MWCNTs–EDAS–AuNPs (EDAS: N-[3(trimethoxysilyl)propyl]ethylenediamine) [10], MWCNTs/mesocellular graphene foam [11], boron-doped-MWCNTs [12], β-cyclodextrin-MWCNTs/poly(luminol)-AuNPs [13], AuNPs/MWCNTs [14], polymeric ionic liquids-MWCNTs [15], which promote electron-transfer, minimize surface fouling and enhance electrocatalytic activity to detect DA. In addition, the π state in the CDs is derived from the SP2 hybridization in the carbon core and the presence of π-π stacking force in the CDs-MWCNTs composites through large conjugated π bonds promotes the electron transfer of DA [16,17]. Meanwhile, DA itself is a special aromatic ring that also possesses π-π stacking force, which can enhance the interaction forces and electron interchanges among MWCNTs, CDs and DA [7]. Furthermore, at neutral pH, the favorable electrostatic interactions between the oxygen-containing functional groups on the surface of CDs, which are usually negatively charged, and the positively charged DA (pKa = 8.87) renders the CD-MWCNTs composite an appropriate sensing material of DA, thereby facilitating more sensitive electrochemical detection of DA. Currently, the synthesis methods of CDs are mainly divided into two categories: top-down (electrolysis, acid etching, laser etching, and high temperature pyrolysis) and bottom-up (chemical synthesis, hydrothermal synthesis, solvothermal method, etc.). However, these methods have some problems, such as cumbersome synthesis steps, long reaction time, low yield or complex purification of CDs, and easy agglomeration of CDs.

In this paper, we proposed a strategy of CDs synthesis under ambient conditions for fast, power-saving, and green synthesis by liquid-phase dielectric barrier discharge (DBD) microplasma. During the synthesis process, no additional strong acid, strong base or toxic chemical substances are added. Microplasma is a millimeter-level low-temperature plasma with a high density of electrons limited in a limited space, which allows the thermal dissociation of molecular gases to form high concentrations of reactive radicals. It does not require high-temperature conditions and a vacuum system [18,19] that has been used for the rapid synthesis of various functional nanomaterials [20,21]. We have previously reported the synthesis of multiple nanomaterials via this liquid-phase DBD microplasma, such as metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [22], covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [23] and other transition metal compounds with specific morphologies [24,25]. Combined with the excellent electrical conductivity of MWCNTs, a composite carbon nanomaterial electrode of CDs/MWCNTs was constructed. As a DA biosensor, the prepared electrode exhibits good sensing performance with a linear range of 2.0–100 μM, the limit of detection (LOD) of 11.08 nM (S/N = 3) and a sensitivity of 29020 μA cm−2 mM−1. This DA sensor has excellent selectivity, and was successfully employed to determine actual blood DA samples.
Reagents, materials, characterization, construction of the liquid DBD microplasma instrument, and electrochemical instruments required for the experiments were in the supplementary information.

Synthesis of CDs and CDs/MWCNTs and CDs/MWCNTs/GCE. Synthesis of CDs: CDs were synthesized by using the DBD method (at 90 V with 60 min). In brief, 4 mL of glycerol was heated in a DBD tube to get a dark yellow solution. After the reaction was completed, it was naturally cooled at room temperature. The cuvette containing the yellow solution appeared blue after irradiation at 365 nm in the dark box. The yellow solution was purified by dialysis with ultrapure water 1:1 at room temperature for 24 h for subsequent experiments.
Synthesis of CDs/MWCNTs: 5 mg of MWCNTs have added to CDs dialysate (980 μL) and 5% naphthol (20 μL), and sonicated for 30 min at room temperature to prepare a uniform CDs/MWCNTs black solution.

Preparation of CDs/MWCNTs modified GCE: Firstly, GCE was mechanically polished by alumina powder with different particle sizes (1.0, 0.3, and 0.05 μm), and then ultrasonically washed with ethanol and water for 3 min, respectively. Then, CDs/MWCNTs/GCE was prepared by dropwise adding 5 μL of CDs/MWCNTs dispersion on the surface of GCE and drying at room temperature for 30 min. Scheme 1 presents a simple illustration of the CDs synthesis and CDs/MWCNTs/GCE fabrication process.

CDs material characterization. Fig. 1 a-c shows the morphologies of CDs at different scales under TEM. The CDs are evenly distributed and not precisely the same size with a difference of 1–2 nm in size. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern (Fig. 1d) shows that the circled red ring can be assigned to the (002) plane of CDs, which all indicate that the synthesis of CDs is successful. Meanwhile, Fig. 1e shows that the positions of the UV absorption maximum (348 nm) and the fluorescence excitation peak (369 nm) are close. The CDs solution shows bright blue light under the UV light excited at 365 nm, which corresponds to the fluorescence emission spectrum at 449 nm (Fig. 1e). Besides, the FT-IR spectra of the CDs shows that a broad absorption band appears at 3406 cm−1 (Fig. S1), corresponding to the ν(O–H) bond. The stretching vibrations of the C–H bond of methyl and methylene appears around 2941 cm−1. Importantly, this peak appears in 1649 cm−1 could belong to the ν(CC), indicating that intermolecular dehydration may occur [26]. Under liquid-phase DBD plasma, glycerol undergoes dehydration, pyrolysis and cross-linking, resulting in nano-sized carbon particles, which may have some oxygen atoms on the surface [27]. The two strong absorption bands at 1043 and 1109 cm−1 are caused by the C–O bond's symmetric and asymmetric stretching modes. These FT-IR absorption peaks indicate the presence of unsaturated carbon and oxygen-added groups such as hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs, and the negatively charged CDs have favorable electrostatic interactions with the positively charged DA at neutral pH, which facilitates the detection of DA.

Cycle voltammetry analysis for DA. The sensing properties of the prepared electrodes are closely related to the oxidizing ability of detectors. Fig. 2 a compares cyclic voltammetry (CV) testing with MWCNTs and CDs/MWCNTs modified GCE without and with 0.1 mM DA. The CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrode with 0.1 mM DA (green curve) shows an oxidation peak current density (Jpa) of 1.17 (mA cm−2) at Epa of 218 mV with the comparison of the Jpa (0.46 mA cm−2) of MWCNTs/GCE (red curve) at Epa of 214 mV. It can be seen that the Jpa of DA oxidation of CDs/MWCNTs/GCE is 2.54 times higher than that of MWCNTs/GCE. On the other hand, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is an effective method for investigating electrode materials' electrochemical properties. The diameter of the semicircular part is equal to the electron transport resistance. The EIS results shown in Fig. S2, the CDs/MWCNTs/GCE exhibited a depressed semicircle with the charge transfer resistance (Rct) value of 11 Ω when compared with MWCNTs/GCE (54 Ω), which indicates that CDs/MWCNTs acts as an excellent conducting material and accelerate the electron transfer process. In addition, the DA-dependent response of the CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrode was investigated. Fig. 2b shows that the Jpa of DA increases uniformly with increasing DA concentration from 0 to 0.5 mM. This further indicates that the modified electrode has an excellent performance in catalyzing the oxidation of DA.

Impact of pH. Epa and Jpa are closely related to the pH of the buffer solution, so the electrochemical behavior of 0.1 mM DA at different pH values (6.2–7.8) in 0.1 M PBS solution was studied by CV. The highest Jpa is obtained at pH 7.4, as shown in Fig. 2c. Therefore, 0.1 M PBS buffer solution at pH 7.4 was used as the standard electrolyte solution for the subsequent detection of DA. Meanwhile, Fig. 2d indicates that the Epa of DA shifted negatively with increasing pH, and the related linear regression equation was: Epa (V) = −0.0586 pH + 0.613 (R2 = 0.9900). According to the following Nernst equation:
(1) Epa = E0 + (0.0591/n) • log[(ox)a/(R)b] – (0.0591 m/n) pH
where m is the number of protons and n refers the number of electrons. The calculated m/n of the DA oxidation is 0.99, showing that the protons and electrons involved in DA oxidation are equal to 2. Hence, the oxidation product of DA is the corresponding quinone [28], as shown in Scheme 1.

Influence of scan rate. Fig. 2e shows the CV curves of different scan rates (ν) between 20 and 200 mV s−1 with the concentration of DA is 0.1 mM. A linear increase in Jp with ν and Fig. 2f shows linear relationships between Jp and ν: Jpa = 0.017ν - 0.019(R2 = 0.9983) and Jpc = −0.017ν + 0.108(R2 = 0.9984), indicating that the oxidation of DA on the prepared electrode is an adsorption-controlled process. The surface coverage of the electrode can be estimated according to the formula using Eq. (2) [29–32]:
(2) Ip = n2F2AΓν/4RT
(3) Jp = n2F2Γν/4RT
Where Γ is the concentration of the redox active adsorbed, Ip is the peak current, n is the number of electron transfers, F is the Faraday, A is the test electrode area, R is the universal gas constant, and T is the Kelvin temperature. During actual experiments, A = 0.07 cm2, n = 2 and equation (3) was obtained from equation (2) through the conversion of Jp = Ip/A, which corresponds to the drawing method in this paper. According to the linear slope of Jp vs. ν, the average Γ on CDs/MWCNTs/GCE is approximately 4.51 × 10−9 mol cm−2, which exceeds the 8.385 × 10−10 mol cm−2 reported based on MWCNTs for DA detection [33], suggesting that the CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrode could provide more DA adsorption sites.

Differential pulse voltammetry behavior of DA. The electrochemical sensing performance of the fabricated CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrode in DA determination was investigated by differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) (Fig. 3 a). As shown in Fig. 3b, the linear equation could be represented as Jpa (mA cm−2) = 29.02 CDA (mM) +1.413 (R2 = 0.9934) for the concentrations from 2 to 100 μM. The sensitivity and LOD are calculated to be 29020 μA cm−2 mM−1and 11.08 nM, respectively. Table 1 compares with other DA sensors based on MWCNTs in terms of linear range and LOD values. By comparison, it is seen that this prepared sensor has a smaller LOD and a proper detection range for DA detection. This further proves that the electrode has good DA detection performance.

Selectivity of CDs/MWCNTs/GCE. It is well known that DA always coexists with AA and UA in biological fluids and their Epa values are very close. Therefore, it is necessary to detect the interference of AA and UA when detecting DA. As shown in Fig. 3c, the change in Jpa values is negligible for 5 different AA, UA and DA ratios in contrast to the Jpa of the pure DA on the CDs/MWCNTs/GCE. Furthermore, Fig. S3 shows that Epa appears around 0.042 and 0.35 V for AA and UA at the CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrodes, respectively, while the Epa of DA occurs at ∼0.18 V, which suppresses the interference of AA and UA on the peak overlap of DA. The difference in Epa positions may be due to the negative charges caused by the presence of multiple oxygen-containing functional groups on the CDs surface, DA is positively charged at physiological pH (pKa ≈8.9), which positive and negative electrostatic interactions increase selectivity for DA, whereas AA (pKa = 4.2) and UA (pKa = 5.4) are negatively charged. In addition, Fig. S4 shows considerable selectivity to 10 μM DA in the presence of 100-fold other biologically active ions such as Na+、K+、Ca2+、Mg2+、Cu2+、Fe2+、Cl−、NO3−、SO4 2− each 1 mM, implying that the superior anti-interference performance of CDs/MWCNTs/GCE toward DA.

Reproducibility of CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrodes. Fig. 3d shows the sensing performance of 15 different CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrodes prepared under the same conditions for 0.1 mM DA through DPV method to test the reproducibility of the electrode. A relative standard deviation (RSD) was only 1.56% (n = 15), confirming the high reproducibility of CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrode preparation.

Detection of DA in human serum. To verify the reliability of the proposed method for analyzing DA in real samples, the recovery of DA in human serum was determined by the standard addition method with CDs/MWCNTs/GCE electrode. The biological sample obtained was a 5-fold dilution of the serum sample with 0.1 M PBS at pH 7.4, in which the specific operation was 200 μL of serum diluted to 1 mL as a biological sample. Then, experimental samples containing different concentrations of DA were prepared from the diluted serum. The experimental results are shown in Table 2 , the recovery rate is between 94.09 and 102.17%, and the RSD is 1.45–4.70%, indicating that the proposed DA sensor has broad application prospects in monitoring the serum samples.

In conclusion, we successfully synthesized CDs by liquid phase DBD microplasma using glycerol as the sole carbon source. Combined with the excellent electrical conductivity of MWCNTs, a composite carbon nanomaterial electrode of CDs/MWCNTs was constructed for DA detection in PBS solution, which exhibited good sensing performance with a linear range of 2.0–100 μM, LOD of 11.08 nM (S/N = 3) and sensitivity of 29020 μA cm−2 mM−1. The improved sensing performance may be attributed to the π−π interactions between CDs, MWCNTs, and the aromatic rings of DA. The sensor successfully detected DA concentration in human serum samples with satisfactory recovery rates. This green and efficient preparation method provides a reference for preparing other carbon-based nanomaterials for electrochemical sensors.

Jiaxin Zhou: Investigation, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yi Xia: Investigation, Methodology, Validation. Zhirong Zou: Investigation, Project administration. Qing Yang: Validation, Project administration. Xue Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition, Supervision, Project administration. Xiaoli Xiong: Writing – review & editing, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22104103), and the Sichuan Department of Science and Technology Program of China (No. 2022YFG0312) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2022.340631.

Sci. Eng. C 58 (2016) 532-540.

Zeng, Selective detection of dopamine in the presence of ascorbic acid by use of glassy-carbon electrodes modified with both polyaniline film and multi-walled carbon nanotubes with incorporated β-cyclodextrin, Anal. Bioanal. Chem. 386(7) (2006) 2087-2094.
